title: Why You Feel Safer in Debt Than Out of It
body:
Why You Feel Safer in Debt Than Out of It

Here's the thing: you're probably more comfortable in debt than you'll ever admit. It’s a gut punch, I know. You might even be bristling right now, thinking, "No way, Kalesh! I hate debt! It's suffocating!" And I hear you. On a conscious level, you absolutely want out. You dream of financial freedom, of not checking your bank account balance with a knot in your stomach. But beneath that conscious desire, there's a deeper, more primal part of you that sees debt as a kind of twisted security blanket. A familiar cage, maybe, but a cage nonetheless. You've built a whole identity around managing debt, haven't you? Around the struggle, the hustle, the constant feeling of "not enough." It's a narrative you know inside and out. It’s predictable. And predictability, even when it's painful, feels safer to your nervous system than the vast, unknown territory of true financial abundance. That’s why the idea of being completely debt-free, with a solid savings cushion, can feel terrifying. It’s like stepping off a cliff, even if the cliff is made of anxiety and restriction. The money wound runs deeper than your budget spreadsheet. It’s not just about numbers on a page. It’s about your earliest experiences with scarcity, with security, with love and belonging. It’s about the stories your parents told you, explicitly or implicitly, about money. It’s about cultural narratives of struggle and deservingness. All of this shapes your relationship with money, creating patterns that are incredibly difficult to break, even when they’re clearly not serving you. Think about it. When you're in debt, there's always something to blame. Always a reason you can't quite "get ahead." Always a convenient excuse for not pursuing that dream, for not taking that risk. Debt becomes a shield. A comfortable excuse. And while it feels awful, it also protects you from the even scarier prospect of having no excuses, of facing your true potential, of realizing that the only thing holding you back was your own unconscious attachment to struggle. You feel that? It’s heavy.

The Unseen Comfort of the Known Struggle

It’s counterintuitive, isn’t it? To find comfort in something that causes so much stress. But our brains are wired for survival, and survival often means sticking with what's known, even if it's suboptimal. Imagine you've spent years working through a dense, dark forest. You know where the thorny bushes are, where the ground is uneven, where the lurking dangers might be. It's not pleasant, but you've developed skills to survive it. Now, imagine someone tells you there's a beautiful, sunlit meadow just beyond the treeline. Sounds amazing, right? But to get there, you have to leave the forest. You have to step into the unknown. All your survival mechanisms, honed by years in the dark, will scream at you to stay put. The known struggle feels safer than the unknown possibility. Debt is that dark forest for many of us. We've become experts at managing it. We know the ins and outs of minimum payments, balance transfers, the tightrope walk of making ends meet. There's a perverse sense of mastery in it, even if it's mastery over scarcity. Dave Ramsey, bless his heart, tries to yank people out of this forest with an axe. "Debt is evil! Get rid of it!" he shouts. And yes, from a purely mathematical standpoint, he's right. But he misses the real psychological and spiritual layers. He misses the fact that for many, debt isn't just a financial problem; it's a symptom of a deeper wound. You can hack away at the branches all you want, but if the roots are still entangled in fear and unworthiness, the struggle will just manifest elsewhere. The poverty consciousness doesn't care how much you earn. You can double your income, and if that underlying consciousness is still present, you'll find new, more sophisticated ways to create debt, to feel insecure, to experience "not enough." It’s a pattern, a vibration, a way of being that money simply reflects. Know what I mean? It's not about the dollar amount; it's about your internal operating system.

The Identity of the "Struggler"

We derive a lot of our identity from our struggles. "I'm the one who always works hard but never gets ahead." "I'm the one who's always sacrificing." "I'm the one who has to be careful with every penny." These aren't just descriptions; they become core parts of who you believe yourself to be. And when you start to shed the debt, when you start to accumulate savings, that identity is challenged. Who are you if you're not the "struggler"? This is where it gets really uncomfortable. Because letting go of debt isn't just about financial numbers; it's about shedding an old skin. It's about stepping into a new version of yourself that you haven't fully met yet. And that can be terrifying. It brings up questions like: If I'm not struggling, will people still like me? Will I still be relatable? Will I lose my edge? Will I become complacent? These fears, often unconscious, keep you tethered to the familiar pain of debt. Bari Tessler, in her work with "The Art of Money" (you should check out her book, it's brilliant), talks about conscious money practices, about bringing mindfulness to your finances. It's not just about the spreadsheets; it's about the feelings, the beliefs, the stories. She understands that your money life is a reflection of your inner life. You can't just fix the surface problem without addressing the underlying emotional and energetic patterns. It's like trying to patch a leaky boat with duct tape when the real issue is a crack in the hull.

Inherited Financial Trauma and the Comfort of Repetition

This isn't just about *your* struggles. Often, it's about the struggles that came before you. Family patterns around money are incredibly powerful. If your parents or grandparents lived through scarcity, through economic downturns, through the constant anxiety of not having enough, those patterns can be passed down. Not just through stories, but through epigenetic changes, through the very fabric of your being. You might be carrying the financial trauma of generations, even if you’ve never experienced true poverty yourself. Mark Wolynn's book, It Didn't Start with You (paid link), explores how unresolved trauma from our ancestors can manifest in our lives today, including in our relationship with money. You might be unconsciously repeating patterns of debt or scarcity because it feels familiar, because it's what your family "did." It's an unconscious loyalty. Does that land? It's not your fault, but it is your responsibility to become aware of it. Think about it. If your lineage has always been "the struggling ones," "the ones who never quite got ahead," then achieving true financial freedom might feel like a betrayal. Like you're leaving your family behind. This is deep, subconscious stuff. It’s not rational. But it’s incredibly powerful. And until you bring it to light, it will continue to exert its influence, keeping you in that familiar, albeit painful, cycle of debt.

The Fear of Having (and Losing) It All

What happens if you actually get out of debt? What if you save a significant amount of money? For many, this brings up an even deeper fear: the fear of losing it. The fear of being robbed, of a market crash, of an unforeseen illness that wipes it all out. The fear of "having it all" only to have it taken away. This fear can be so paralyzing that it's actually "safer" to stay in a state of mild scarcity, where there's less to lose. Krishnamurti spoke extensively about the nature of fear and attachment. He argued that true freedom comes from observing these attachments without judgment, understanding their root. When you're attached to the idea of safety through scarcity, you're actually creating the very conditions you fear. You're constantly reinforcing the belief that you're not safe, that you can't trust abundance. Kate Northrup, author of Money: A Love Story (paid link), encourages a more loving, gentle approach to money. She understands that money is just energy with a story attached to it. If your story is one of fear and lack, then that's the energy you'll attract and perpetuate. If you can shift your story, you can shift your energetic relationship with money. But this requires courage. It requires facing those deep-seated fears of loss and embracing a new, more expansive possibility.

The False Security of Constant Debt Payments

There's a strange rhythm to debt. The monthly payments become a kind of anchor. A consistent, if unwelcome, presence in your life. You might even feel a sense of accomplishment when you make those payments, even though they're merely keeping you afloat, not truly moving you forward. This constant cycle of paying and feeling the pressure can create a sense of false security. "At least I'm making progress," you tell yourself, even if the needle isn't moving much. What happens when those payments disappear? When that monthly obligation is gone? There's a void. And voids can be uncomfortable. For some, that void is quickly filled with new spending, new debt. For others, it's filled with anxiety. "What do I do with this extra money? What if I make a mistake?" This is where the real work begins. It's not just about eliminating debt; it's about learning how to *be* with abundance, how to trust yourself with it, how to manage it from a place of peace, not panic. This is the deeper healing. It's about recognizing that true security doesn't come from having debt or not having debt. It comes from within. From your sense of self-worth, your belief in your ability to create and contribute, your trust in the flow of life. Debt is merely a reflection of where you are in that journey.

The Path to True Financial Freedom: It's an Inside Job

So, how do you break free from this unconscious attachment to debt? How do you move from the known struggle to the unknown abundance? It starts with awareness. You can't change what you don't acknowledge. First, be honest with yourself. Really honest. Do you secretly, unconsciously, find comfort in the struggle? Does debt serve as an excuse for something else in your life? Do you identify with being "the one who always struggles"? No judgment. Just observe. Second, begin to question your inherited money stories. What did your family teach you about money? What beliefs did you absorb about wealth, poverty, deservingness? Write them down. See how they might be playing out in your life today. Third, practice radical self-compassion. This isn't about shaming yourself for past choices or current situations. It's about recognizing that you're operating from deep, often unconscious, patterns. Treat yourself with the same kindness you'd offer a dear friend. Brené Brown's work on vulnerability and shame, particularly in Sacred Success (paid link), can be incredibly helpful here. She helps you understand that vulnerability isn't weakness; it's courage. And facing your money shame is one of the most courageous acts you can undertake. Fourth, start small. Don't try to overhaul your entire financial life overnight. Pick one small debt to focus on. Celebrate every tiny win. Shift your internal narrative from "I'm always in debt" to "I'm on a journey to financial freedom, one step at a time." Finally, cultivate a sense of worthiness that is independent of your bank account balance. You are worthy of abundance, not because you've paid off all your debts, but because you exist. Because you are a divine being. This is the spiritual foundation of true financial freedom. Money is just energy with a story attached to it. What story are you telling? And what new story are you ready to write?

FAQ

Why do I keep getting into debt even when I make more money?

This is a classic sign that you're dealing with a deeper money wound, not just a budget problem. The poverty consciousness doesn't care how much you earn. If your underlying beliefs about scarcity, unworthiness, or the need to struggle are still active, you'll unconsciously find ways to create debt, regardless of your income. It's about your internal operating system, not just the external numbers.

Is it really possible to feel safe without debt?

Absolutely, but it's a process. Your nervous system has been conditioned to associate debt with a twisted kind of familiarity and predictability. Moving into true financial freedom means retraining your brain to feel safe in abundance. This involves addressing inherited financial trauma, questioning your identity as a "struggler," and building a deep sense of inner worthiness that isn't tied to your financial situation. It's an inside job.

How do I start to identify my "money wound"?

Start by observing your thoughts and feelings around money without judgment. What's your immediate reaction when you check your bank balance? What stories did your parents tell you about money? What's your biggest fear related to money - is it losing it, not having enough, or something else? Pay attention to recurring patterns in your spending or saving habits. Journaling about these observations can be incredibly insightful.

What if I'm afraid of what people will think if I become wealthy or debt-free?

This is a very common, often unconscious, fear. It goes back to the identity of the "struggler" and the fear of leaving your "tribe" behind. You might worry about judgment, jealousy, or becoming unrelatable. Acknowledge these fears. Understand that true friends will celebrate your success, and anyone who doesn't is reflecting their own scarcity issues. Your financial freedom doesn't diminish anyone else's; it can actually inspire them.

Your challenge today: Identify one small, specific money belief you hold that keeps you in the struggle. Just one. Write it down. Then, for the next 24 hours, simply observe how that belief shows up in your thoughts, feelings, and actions. No need to change it yet. Just watch it. That's the first step to freedom.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Discover why debt can feel like a safety net. Understand the subconscious reasons you might cling to it, even while desiring financial freedom. Explore this sur
author: Kalesh
